title: Dried Blood on Carpet
timeEstimate: 45–90 minutes home triage; aged + heat-set = pro / restoration territory
stainChemistry: Dried blood is the harder case — the protein has already set and the iron has turned the stain from red to brown. The brown stays even after the protein lifts because it's iron oxide, not protein. On carpet, two extra complications stack: the carpet pad below absorbs blood that soaks through (saturation = harder cleanup, possible mildew), and aggressive scrubbing on dyed carpet leaves a halo. Two hard rules: NO hot water (locks anything still loose) and NO saturation (drives blood into the pad where you can't reach it). Multi-pass is the norm on dried blood — expect 2 to 3 cycles minimum.
whyThisWorks: Cold-water moistening softens the dried protein crust without setting it deeper. An enzyme pretreat (cool water only) breaks down the residual protein over 20 to 30 minutes. Dawn handles any oily film. 3% peroxide on a damp cloth (hidden test on dyed carpet) handles the iron-brown shadow that's left after the protein is gone. Weighted towels overnight prevent wick-back from the pad. The honest cap: aged blood + hot vacuum / hot dryer / steam already used = often a permanent shadow; pro / restoration is the right call.
scienceNote: Mechanism (pro-facing): dried blood combines set protein matrix + Fe³⁺ iron-based pigment. Pro uses cold liquid Protein Spotter (protease pretreat at controlled dilution) plus NSD plus sodium hydrosulfite for set/oxidized iron component — reducing bleach is more effective than peroxide on the iron-brown shadow.
defaultAssumption: Cut, kid scrape, period stain, or mishap on carpet that wasn't caught fresh — sat overnight or longer and dried in. Synthetic (nylon / polyester / olefin) carpet = home protocol candidate. Wool carpet = pro from minute one (pet-urine-wool-carpet rules apply: no broad-spectrum enzymes, cold-only). Visible bleed-through to the pad = restoration / pro.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Gloves on (biohazard). Wool carpet = pro from minute one. Press the carpet — if the pad below is damp / shows visible blood / smells musty, stop and call a pro. Synthetic carpet with surface dried blood = home candidate.
Crack the dried crust with the flat edge of a plastic card. HEPA vacuum the loose flakes. Don't rub or grind into the pile.

Damp white cloth with COLD water — wrung nearly dry. Press on the spot, let sit 10 min to soften the dried crust. Damp-cloth only — never pour water (sinks blood into the pad). Lift, rotate to clean section, press again.

spottingProtocol.equipment: OxiClean Versatile / Biz / pet enzyme in COOL water on damp cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dissolve enzyme pretreat in COOL water per label. Apply with damp cloth — never pour. Cover with a damp towel to prevent drying. 20–30 min dwell. Cool damp-cloth rinse.
Hidden carpet area: dab 3% peroxide, blot with white cloth. Color transfers = skip peroxide. Clean = proceed.

Hidden-test-passed synthetic carpet only. 3% peroxide damp cloth 5–10 min for residual iron-brown shadow. Damp-cloth rinse. NEVER mix with chlorine bleach, ammonia, or alkaline cleaners. Skip on wool carpet.

spottingProtocol.agent: Weighted Paper Towels Overnight Plus Fan
spottingProtocol.instruction: Stack paper towels on the spot, weight overnight. Fan the room. Prevents brown pigment wicking back up from the pad as the carpet dries. Inspect dry — multi-pass is the norm; repeat enzyme + peroxide if shadow remains.

steps: 1. PPE + pad assessment. Wool carpet / pad bleed-through = pro / restoration.
2. Plastic-edge crack and HEPA vacuum dried crust.
4. Enzyme pretreat in COOL water on damp cloth, 20–30 min.
5. Hidden-area peroxide test on dyed carpet.
6. 3% H₂O₂ damp cloth for iron-brown shadow on hidden-test-passed synthetic carpet.
7. Weighted paper towels overnight + fan. Multi-pass expected.
products: Enzyme pretreat (OxiClean Versatile / Biz / pet enzyme)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool throughout — heat sets residual blood and iron-brown
warnings: NEVER hot water — sets the protein and iron-brown shadow permanently
NEVER saturate the carpet — drives blood into the pad
NEVER chlorine bleach panic moves on carpet — strips carpet dye, doesn't reach pad blood
NEVER alcohol on blood — sets the protein
Wool carpet = pro from minute one
Pad contamination = restoration, not stain cleaning
Hidden peroxide test mandatory on dyed carpet
Multi-pass expected — 2 to 3 cycles is normal
Biohazard — gloves required, sealed disposal
Never mix peroxide with bleach / ammonia / alkaline

homeSolutions.agent: Dried Blood Is Multi-Pass — Set Expectations
homeSolutions.instruction: Dried blood on carpet rarely comes out in one cleaning cycle. Plan on 2 to 3 passes minimum. Two hard rules: NO hot water (sets the protein and iron-brown shadow permanently) and NO saturation (drives blood into the pad below where you can't reach it). Gloves on — biohazard, especially if it's not your blood.

homeSolutions.agent: Wool Carpet Goes to Pro From Minute One
homeSolutions.instruction: If your carpet is wool — pro from minute one. Wool's banned-tools list (no broad-spectrum enzymes, no peroxide, no agitation) eliminates everything that lifts dried blood from synthetic carpet. Synthetic carpet (nylon, polyester, olefin) = home protocol candidate.

Press the carpet down with your hand. If the pad below feels damp, has visible blood bleed-through, or smells musty, STOP — that's pad contamination, not a stain job. Call a pro. The pad has to come up; it can't be cleaned in place.

homeSolutions.agent: Crack the Crust and HEPA Vacuum
homeSolutions.instruction: Use the flat edge of a plastic card to crack the dried blood crust. HEPA vacuum the loose flakes (regular vacuum spreads them; HEPA traps them). Don't rub or grind.

Damp a white cloth with COLD water — wring it nearly dry. Press on the spot, let it sit 10 minutes to soften the dried crust. Lift, rotate to a clean section, press again. NEVER pour water on the carpet — pour-on saturation sends blood into the pad where it grows mildew.

Dissolve an enzyme pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or a pet-specific enzyme) in COOL water per the label. Apply with a damp cloth — never pour. Cover with a damp towel so it doesn't dry out. 20 to 30 minutes. Damp-cloth rinse with cool water. Don't be tempted to use warm water 'to help' — heat above bath temperature damages the enzymes AND sets any remaining protein.
In a hidden carpet area, dab 3% peroxide on a white cloth, press 2 minutes. Carpet color transfers = skip peroxide entirely. No transfer = proceed.

Hidden-test-passed synthetic carpet only: 3% drugstore peroxide on a damp cloth, press the spot 5 to 10 minutes, blot. Handles the iron-brown shadow that's left after the protein is gone. Damp-cloth rinse. NEVER mix with bleach, ammonia, or OxiClean — toxic fumes. Skip entirely on wool carpet.

homeSolutions.agent: Weighted Paper Towels Overnight Plus Fan
homeSolutions.instruction: Stack paper towels on the spot, weight with a heavy book, leave overnight. Fan the room in the morning. This pulls residual moisture and pigment out of the pile and prevents brown wicking back up from the pad. Inspect dry. Faint shadow remaining = repeat the enzyme + peroxide cycle. Multi-pass is normal.

steps: 2. Pad bleed-through / saturation = restoration, not stain cleaning.
3. Crack crust with plastic edge + HEPA vacuum.
4. Cold damp-cloth soften 10 min — never pour.
5. Enzyme pretreat in COOL water on damp cloth, 20–30 min.
6. Hidden-area peroxide test on dyed carpet.
7. 3% peroxide damp cloth for iron-brown shadow on hidden-test-passed synthetic carpet.
8. Weighted paper towels overnight + fan. Repeat 2–3 cycles as needed.
products: Enzyme pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, pet enzyme)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop on wool carpet (pro). Stop on pad contamination — that's restoration. Stop if hot water / steam / hot vacuum was already used (often permanent brown shadow). Stop if hidden-area peroxide test fails on dyed carpet. Stop after 3 home cycles plateau.
warnings: NEVER hot water — sets blood and iron-brown shadow permanently
NEVER saturate carpet — drives blood into the pad
NEVER chlorine bleach on carpet — strips carpet dye, leaves halo
NEVER alcohol on blood
Wool carpet = pro from minute one
Pad contamination = restoration, not home cleaning
Hidden peroxide test on dyed carpet
Multi-pass expected — 2–3 cycles normal
Biohazard — gloves on
Never mix peroxide with bleach / ammonia / alkaline

neverDo: Never hot water on dried blood — sets it permanently
Never saturate carpet — drives blood into pad
Never chlorine bleach on dyed carpet
Never broad-spectrum enzymes on wool carpet
Never bare-handed if blood isn't your own
Never mix peroxide with bleach / ammonia / OxiClean
fiberSensitivities: Synthetic (nylon / polyester / olefin) carpet: tolerates enzyme pretreat + 3% peroxide on hidden-test-passed
Wool carpet: pro from minute one — broad-spectrum enzymes damage wool, peroxide yellows wool
Dyed carpet: hidden peroxide test mandatory
Carpet pad: cannot be cleaned in place — saturation = pad replacement / restoration

materialWarnings: Multi-pass is the norm on dried blood — set expectations
No hot water / no saturation — both lock the stain
Wool carpet = pro from minute one
Hidden peroxide test mandatory on dyed carpet

escalation.when: Wool carpet from minute one. Pad contamination — damp pad, visible bleed-through, musty smell from below. Hot water / steam / hot vacuum already used (often permanent brown shadow). Hidden peroxide test fails on dyed carpet. 3 home cycles plateau. Aged set blood through multiple cleaning attempts.
escalation.whatToTell: "Dried blood on [synthetic / wool] carpet, age [overnight / days / weeks], home-treated with [crack and HEPA vacuum + cool enzyme pretreat × N + 3% peroxide on hidden-test-passed synthetic + weighted towels overnight]. No hot, no saturation, no chlorine bleach, no alcohol. [Residual brown shadow / pad contamination suspected / hot already used]."
escalation.specialistType: professional carpet cleaner with truck-mount sub-surface extraction for synthetic carpet; specialty wool carpet cleaner OR mold remediation specialist if pad contamination

contextQuestions: Synthetic (nylon / polyester / olefin) carpet or wool?
Has hot water / steam / hot vacuum been used yet?
Does the pad below feel damp or smell musty?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Dried blood is multi-pass — manage expectations. The two hard pad-contamination signals (damp pad, musty smell from below) move this from a stain job to a restoration job. Hot vacuum / steam pre-used is the most common home failure mode because it sets the protein. Wool carpet has its own protocol (pet-urine-wool-carpet rules apply: cold only, no broad-spectrum enzymes, no peroxide). The iron-brown shadow that remains after protein removal is a separate sub-stain — peroxide handles it on synthetic, sodium hydrosulfite is the pro tool on more delicate fibers.
